data.title: My Top Near 10% Yield Pick For July 2025: Western Midstream
data.description:
10% yields often come with major yellow and even red flags for me. This is because whenever a stock yields 10%, it typically means at least one of two things. Either A, the market does not expect that yield to be sustainable and therefore is pricing it at a higher yield in order to compensate for an expected dividend cut in the not-too-distant future, or B, expects it to generate practically no growth over the long term. This is because the 10% yield already provides a baseline equity-like return. So if it were to generate any sort of meaningful growth on top of that, it would make it an outperforming stock. Therefore, if Mr. Market is going to award a security a 10% yield, it likely does not expect much in the way of growth beyond that.

This is true in the vast majority of cases, especially in segments like mortgage REITs (MORT), among which even the most blue-chip, such as Arbor Realty Trust (ABR), recently cut its dividend for the first time in many years, or BDCs (BIZD), where even the most blue-chip, like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), have only been able to grow their base dividends at a slow pace and inconsistently over the long term. Additionally, you can buy lower-quality preferreds and bonds at near 10% yields, but these, in addition to offering higher than normal risk, offer nothing in the way of growth from underlying earnings, given that they are fixed-income investments, and in many cases require taking on significant margin leverage (e.g., the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA)).

However, there is one opportunity right now that yields ~10% that not only looks sustainable for years to come, but also has inflation-beating growth potential and is backed by a very defensive and durable business model, as well as an investment-grade balance sheet. In this article, I am going to detail why Western Midstream Partners (NYSE:WES) is my top 10% yielding pick right now.

Balance Sheet

First and foremost, the most important aspects of assessing why WES is a great purchase are to look at the strength of its balance sheet, and the durability of its business model. In terms of the balance sheet, it has an investment-grade credit rating, which signals that the ratings agencies have a fairly strong degree of confidence in its solvency. In addition, it generates a lot of free cash flow, with management guiding for between $1.3 and $1.5 billion worth of free cash flow in 2025, which gives it significant financial flexibility to be able to opportunistically pay down debt and/or invest in the business as it sees fit while still being able to support its hefty distribution.

In addition to its investment-grade credit rating from S&P, Fitch, and Moody's, WES has one of the lowest leverage ratios in the midstream space (AMLP) with a 2.96x leverage ratio. This is notably even lower than Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and MPLX (MPLX), which are two other conservatively postured midstream businesses. Its debt maturities and liquidity are also well-positioned, as it has nearly $2.5 billion worth of liquidity on hand, with only a total of $777 million worth of debt maturing this year and next year. Meanwhile, it has no debt maturing in 2027, and it has 80% of its debt not maturing until 2029 or later, which gives it very well-laddered debt maturities. Put together, this all means that WES has a very low probability of experiencing financial distress for the foreseeable future, which helps to support the durability of its 10% yield as well as management's ability to continue growing that payout.

Business Model

Another reason to really like WES's yield durability is the fact that its business model is defensive and durable. From a big picture perspective, the ongoing AI boom and the reshoring dynamic being pushed by the Trump administration mean that there should be significant demand for years to come for domestically produced energy. This, in turn, supports U.S.-focused energy infrastructure businesses in particular due to the fact that the Trump administration has taken a much more favorable regulatory posture towards building energy production and infrastructure facilities.

WES's portfolio is also well-diversified with 21 gathering systems, 77 processing and treating facilities, 7 natural gas pipelines, 11 crude oil and NGL pipelines, and over 14,000 miles of pipelines.

The business also has a very commodity price-resistant and defensive cash flow profile, with 95% of its cash flow coming from fee-based gas contracts and 100% fee-based liquids contracts. It also has minimum volume commitments or cost of service protections on its production, giving it a very stable commodity price-resistant posture.

Additionally, when looking at its underlying exposure in the Delaware Basin, its weighted average remaining contract term is about nine years for gas, over seven years for oil, and over eight years for water. In the D.J. Basin, on its gas assets, it has 88% of its gas production with a four-year weighted average remaining term on its contracts, with the remaining 12% having a life-of-lease contract. Its oil production in the D.J. Basin has a four-year weighted average remaining term on its contracts.

Meanwhile, WES also has an extensive list of equity investments that it can liquidate (with the exception being its 50% stake in the Mentone plant in West Texas operated by Energy Transfer (ET), which is tied into its system and is not for sale) as necessary to unlock capital that it can reinvest in its core business, repurchase units with, or pursue more strategic bolt-on acquisitions with to further create value for unitholders. When you combine its commodity price resistance, its lengthy cash flow profile, its diversified nature, and its premier operating position in the Delaware Basin—which is in the most prolific part of the Delaware Basin, from which it generates the majority of its revenue—WES enjoys a defensive and durable business model underpinning its distribution.

Risk Analysis

One point that some like to make about WES's business model is that they do remain somewhat beholden to Occidental Petroleum (OXY), given that OXY owns 44% of WES's units. Therefore, OXY could pressure WES into taking actions that are contrary to the interests of unitholders or potentially even sell off a major portion of its stake, which could weigh heavily on WES's unit price in the near term.

However, I think this is unlikely for several reasons. First of all, OXY benefits tremendously from the large distributions that WES throws off, which give them significant cash flow that they can use for numerous corporate purposes. Additionally, WES operates some critical infrastructure that would be very expensive to replace for OXY, making it very much a symbiotic relationship between the two. Furthermore, insiders own a considerable amount of equity at WES, making sure that they are well aligned with the unitholders that they represent.

Additionally, having such a large unitholder and counterparty in OXY—of which Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) is a major shareholder—in some ways is actually a strength rather than a weakness, as it provides a pretty strong counterparty to underpin many of WES's contracts, making its cash flow profile even more stable in nature.

Additionally, even if OXY should sell a bunch or even all of its WES units—which it has not indicated it is planning to do; in fact, it has indicated that it plans to be a long-term unitholder in WES—it would simply create an opportunity for WES to repurchase units at extremely compelling value, thereby creating value for long-term unitholders at a higher rate than would be able to through other investment opportunities. Moreover, we would probably buy more units ourselves and lock in much higher yields, as the sale of units would have no bearing on the underlying cash flows and ultimately the intrinsic value of the business. It would simply be a short-term instance of volatility caused by market dynamics.

Distribution & Valuation

As far as the coverage and growth of the distribution goes, it is worth noting that, first of all, WES does not need to invest a ton of capital to still generate solid growth, as it has a historical track record of generating 18% return on assets compared to its peer average of 13%. It has also generated very attractive returns from its bolt-on acquisitions versus its divestitures, as its non-core sales in 2024 were completed at nine times adjusted EBITDA, whereas its recent Meritage acquisition was executed at just a 5.5 times EBITDA multiple.

This, in turn, enables them to return the vast majority of their free cash flow to unitholders, as their current guidance for 2025 suggests that their total distribution outlay will be about $1.375 billion, which stands right near the midpoint of their 2025 EBITDA.

However, that also is accounting for capital expenditures and other growth investments. While WES does not report distributable cash flow per unit itself, according to TIKR.com, the analyst consensus estimate is that WES will generate $4.72 per unit of distributable cash flow in 2025, which would cover the distribution they are expecting to pay out this year by a comfortable 1.3 times, indicating that the distribution is on very sustainable footing. Additionally, analysts are expecting the distributable cash flow per unit to grow at a 6.7% CAGR through 2029, which should be somewhat closely mirrored by free cash flow growth of 5.8% CAGR through 2029. Note that this free cash flow estimate is in total terms, whereas if they were to repurchase units—as they have been—moving forward that CAGR will likely be even higher. This should support solid inflation-beating distribution growth for years to come, and analysts are expecting the distribution to grow at a roughly 4% CAGR through 2029 as well, further confirming this expectation.

When combined with the current yield of nearly 10% on a next-12-month basis and an attractive enterprise value to EBITDA multiple of just 8.9 times, WES not only offers a very attractive current yield that appears to be quite sustainable due to the strength of its balance sheet, its business model, and its cash flow generation, but also offers an attractive total return profile as it should be able to grow its distribution at an inflation-beating rate for years to come while also having a very reasonable valuation multiple that could earn it valuation multiple expansion and further total return upside in the years to come.

Investor Takeaway

Given the defensive and durable business model and investment-grade balance sheet, high sustainable yield, inflation-beating growth profile, relatively low-risk, and attractive valuation multiple, we believe that WES is a phenomenal high yield investment opportunity right now. As a result, it is one of our very largest positions at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Ardelyx: 'Hold' With Caution As Major Inflection Point Approaches
data.description:
Ardelyx Overview

I believe that for now Ardelyx (NASDAQ:ARDX) should remain with a "hold" rating, and that is due to two reasons in particular. The first reason is because its drug XPHOZAH is still growing at a decent rate at the moment, which is 30% year-over-year. The second reason is because of a major inflection point, which is rapidly approaching in September/October of 2025. This would be the potential for it to win an appeals court ruling with respect to the ongoing legal battle with the U.S. government over Medicare coverage for its approved kidney disease drug XPHOZAH.

Oral arguments are expected on September 25th of 2025, according to a released docket schedule. If the company wins this appeal ruling to have this drug put back under Medicare Part D, then it would allow the company to see massive growth with respect to sales of this drug. On the flip side, a negative ruling would move to significantly lower the amount of sales that could be generated with it. With a negative ruling happening, then at that point I would move the stock to a "Sell Rating". But it remains a "hold" for now on the basis that it might actually have a chance to win its appeal of its drug XPHOZAH, which was approved to reduce serum phosphorus in patients with chronic kidney disease [CKD].

XPHOZAH Has Growth But Restoration Of Part D Is Crucial For Long-Term Success

As I noted above, Ardelyx is in the process of selling its FDA-approved drug XPHOZAH to reduce serum phosphorus in patients with chronic kidney disease. Before going over the clinical data that brought it to the point of receiving FDA approval of this drug for the treatment of these patients, plus a major inflection point set to come about, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. Chronic Kidney Disease [CKD] is a type of disorder characterized as the progressive loss of kidney function whereby it can no longer filter blood as well as it should be able to. Two major contributing factors to this disorder are having high blood pressure and diabetes. The early stages of CKD are not so bad, but the problem is that there are patients who reach the end stages of this disorder. At this point, there are only two possible options, and they are as follows:

  • Kidney transplant
  • To be placed on dialysis

The latter bullet point, of needing to be placed on dialysis, is a machine used to mimic an artificial kidney to filter out waste and excess fluid. In the 7 major markets, the chronic kidney disease market is expected to reach $13.7 billion by 2035. This is a very large market opportunity, but it is important to keep a perspective on this. The goal is to target who need to have dialysis to filter out waste in their blood. It is said that between 4.90 and 7.08 million of these patients with end-stage kidney disease [ESKD] need kidney replacement therapy. The thing is that dialysis brings about high levels of phosphorus in the blood [hyperphosphatemia]. In turn, to reduce serum phosphorus levels, patients would take phosphate binders. Consider these phosphate binders as being necessary for the patients who receive dialysis. What's their function? These phosphate binders bind to phosphorus and prevent it from being absorbed into the bloodstream. One major problem, though, is that CKD patients who are receiving dialysis either can't tolerate the phosphate binders they are taking or they have an inadequate response to them. This is where an FDA-approved drug like XPHOZAH comes into play. It has a different mechanism of action [MOA], which is a 3-part function:

  • It specifically inhibits or blocks NHE3 protein, which results in less sodium absorption
  • Modest protein retention between cells
  • Alters tight junctions in paracellular pathways, leading to reduced phosphate absorption

In essence, this provides an alternative way to reduce serum phosphorus in these CKD patients in a safer manner.

Thus, FDA approval of XPHOZAH came about because of meeting key primary and secondary endpoints across three phase 3 studies, known as PHREEDOM, BLOCK, AMPLIFY. The bottom line is that this drug was able to reduce serum phosphorus levels in a statistically significant manner for CKD patients who were receiving maintenance hemodialysis.

A Possible Saving Grace For Sales To Grow Substantially

The drug, XPHOZAH is doing okay in terms of sales. At the very least, at least it is still continuing to see double-digit growth. That is, U.S. net product sales revenue for this drug in Q1 of 2025 was $23.4 million. This ends up being a 30% increase in sales growth from the same time period in the year prior. So from the get-go, sales are not doing too badly to begin with. Now, for the major reason why I believe that Ardelyx should have a "hold" rating. This has to do with a major battle with the U.S. government so that it could have Medicare coverage for its kidney disease therapy XPHOZAH.

It all started back in July of 2024, whereby the company sued the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services. The reason why is because these centers decided to move XPHOZAH to its End-Stage Renal Disease Prospective Payment System [ESRD-PPS]. What's wrong with this? Well, if this holds, then the company will not be able to have this serum phosphorus lowering drug covered under Medicare Part D. In turn, this would greatly reduce the amount of revenue to be generated from sales of it in the coming years. A single judge pretty much caused a major problem for Ardelyx.

However, there is a chance to rectify this move and hopefully restore the company's ability to potentially increase the revenue to be generated from sales of XPHOZAH. In order to counter how the judge ruled in November of 2024, it decided to appeal the decision. This brings us to the other day, whereby there was a docket update, in that there was an oral hearing set for September 25th of 2025 to hear oral arguments. Should the oral arguments go well at that time, plus the company wins its appeal, then it should be able to regain stature on Medicare Part D coverage of XPHOZAH. That would be a huge growth driver going forward and would allow the company to achieve increased sales growth of this drug to reduce serum phosphorus levels for these patients with CKD.

ARDX Stock Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Ardelyx had cash, cash equivalents, and investments of $214 million as of March 31st of 2025. The downside for this company is that it still needs significant cash to continue to operate. This is on the basis of a few reasons. The first reason of which is that it believes that its cash on hand will only be enough to operate its business for at least one year from the date of this 10-Q SEC Filing. The second reason is that it had to raise cash through a few financial transactions.

There was a 2022 loan agreement that it had generated, which totaled $200 million. Out of this entire amount, only $150 million has been drawn upon as of March 31st of 2025. The term loans in place from this are expected to mature on July 1st of 2028. In January of 2023, there was a Form S-3 sales agreement filed, by which the company could offer and sell up to $250 million units from time to time as needed. Thereafter, there was a $150 million at-the-market [ATM] offering agreement established with Jefferies to sell shares as needed.

In Q1 of 2025, there were no sales made from it. However, to date it did sell up to $70 million worth at an average stock price of $4.17 per share. The point here is that there is cash it can tap into if it needs to. Besides the XPHOZAH sales I noted above, the company has also received marketing approval of another drug known as IBSRELA [tenapanor]. This drug was approved for the treatment of adults with irritable bowel syndrome with constipation [IBS-C]. In Q1 of 2025, Ardelyx noted that it had generated $44.4 million in net product sales for it. With that being said, this was a larger year-over-year growth in sales than that of XPHOZAH, growing by 57%.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Ardelyx. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the hearing of oral arguments relating to the lawsuit that it had placed. That is, there was an appeal by the company itself to restore the possibility of it being able to have XPHOZAH sold under Medicare Part D again. The risk here is that the oral arguments laid out on September 25th of 2025 may not be enough to win its appeal of receiving Medicare Part D coverage for its kidney disease therapy. If a negative outcome occurs, then that would greatly hinder its chance at obtaining substantial revenue growth of XPHOZAH going forward.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of IBSRELA, which has seen a substantial year-over-year growth of 57% in Q1 of 2025 to $44.4 million. The risk here is that even though this drug has been performing well for the company in the most recently reported earnings quarter, there is no assurance that the same growth is to be achieved in the coming quarters. Nor that this particular drug will continue to perform well in light of competition being in place. For instance, you have the likes of LINZESS [linaclotide] from Ironwood Pharmaceuticals (IRWD) and AbbVie (ABBV) , TRULANCE [plecantide] from Salix Pharmaceuticals and AMITIZA [lubiprostone]. These are competitors that could hinder the amount of sales that it could generate with IBSRELA.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. As I noted above, despite all of the financial measures it has put in place, it may still not have the adequate funds to carry itself forward. For instance, as denoted from its 10-Q SEC Filing, it believes that it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. This means the risk here is that it may have to tap back into the remaining ATM agreement it has in place with Jefferies, which could further dilute shares of its stock. Either that, or it could possibly need to raise cash by some other means, which would have the same dilutive effect on shareholders.

Conclusion

Ardelyx has done pretty well in terms of sales for its drugs IBSRELA and XPHOZAH. The latter drug is seeing modest double-digit growth at 30%, but the ability for the company to really generate shareholder value would be to win its appeal in terms of restoring Medicare Part D coverage of it. Thus, I would consider the oral arguments on September 25th of 2025 and then the appeal decision as major inflection points, which one or another is going to have a significant impact on its revenue growth of XPHOZAH going forward.

The company is relying on this drug and IBSRELA to generate shareholder value; thus, either drug not performing well in sales or up to par could mean shareholder value being eroded. For the time being, with the fact that there is a glimmer of hope that Ardelyx could win its appeal to retain Medicare Part D coverage, I believe that its stock remains a "hold" rating. The only way I would change my stance is if the appeal fails and no further advancement options are possible to retain this Medicare Part D coverage. At which point, I would move this stock to a "Sell" rating if the appeal does not go as well as it should.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Upstart Has Proven Itself - Current Premium Not Worth It
data.description:
UPST's Profitable/High-Growth Proposition Will Be A Lot More Compelling After A Deep Pullback

We previously covered Upstart Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UPST) in February 2025, discussing how it had demonstrated great resilience and growing relevance in the lending market, despite the challenging macroeconomic environment thus far.

Despite the promising performance metrics, the reduced loans held on the balance sheet, the growing funding liquidity, and the expanding profitability, it was also apparent that the stock had already pulled forward most of its upside potential, with its upward momentum seemingly buoyed by momentum, resulting in our reiterated Hold rating then.

UPST 1Y Stock Price

Since then, UPST has indeed pulled back deeply during the worst of the April 2025 meltdown, with the stock finally trading near our fair value estimates of $39.70, with the same sell-off also observed in its fintech peers in varying degrees.

Even so, thanks to the 90-day tariff pause, the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US and China, the still resilient US labor market, and the muted CPI numbers in May 2025, we can understand why market sentiments have already drastically lifted since the recent selloff.

This is significantly aided by the Fed potentially embarking on two rate cuts by the end of 2025, with it perhaps explaining why numerous fintech stocks have already rallied by the double digits since the recent bottom, UPST included, by the triple digits.

The rally has been further validated by UPST's raised FY2025 GAAP net income guidance from the prior of "at least breakeven" in the FQ4'24 earnings call to "be positive in the second half of the year and positive for the full calendar year," with it building upon the two consecutive quarters of positive adj EPS performance since FQ4'24.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the higher Transaction Volumes of 240.7K loans in FQ1'25 (-2% QoQ/ +101.6% YoY) and the total loan originations of $2.13B (+1.4% QoQ/ +88.4% YoY).

This is on top of the expanded loan conversion rate of 19.1% (-0.2 points QoQ/ +5.1 YoY), with the increased adoption directly triggering the growing revenues from fees of $185.47M (-6.9% QoQ/ +34.3% YoY).

These developments have validated UPST's AI-driven lending platform indeed, as observed in the growing funding availability from over 100 banks, credit unions, and private credit partners, which is also why the management has been able to move more loan funding away from its balance sheet.

This may be aided by the new agreements for its HELOC (home loans) products, as they "begun the process of moving funding off of our balance sheet" while potentially driving increased home loan originations ahead.

Combined with the highly successful cross-selling trends to its existing customers, we believe that the HELOC and auto loan offerings are likely to be UPST's growth drivers in the near term, as observed in their rich sequential growth by +51.8% QoQ/ +485.7% YoY and +41.8% QoQ/ +369.2% YoY, respectively, well balancing the stagnant personal loan growth.

As a result of these promising developments, it is unsurprising that the management has been able to achieve its bottom-line reversal faster than expected, thanks to the efficient consumer acquisition trends, the growing loan conversion rates, and the improved operational scale.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These have directly triggered the consensus raised forward estimates, with the UPST expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +32.3%/ +35.72% (based on the annualized FQ1'25 adj EPS of $1.20 and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $3.00) through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +24.3%/ +5.7% (a year ago) and the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +31.1%, underscoring their robust execution despite the challenging past few years of macro/ geopolitical uncertainties and the next few years of normalization.

UPST Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has upgraded UPST's FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations to 37.60x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 45.52x, up from the 1Y mean of 103.51x/ 40.72x, the sector median of 10.88x/ 11.11x, and its fintech peers, including:

  • Block, Inc. (XYZ) at 11.91x/ 24.95x,
  • Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) at 322.20x/ 29.37x,
  • SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) at NA/ 63.23x,
  • LendingClub Corporation (LC) at 3.41x/ 19.19x,
  • PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) at 10.65x/ 14.79x, respectively,

demonstrating the former's rather premium valuations.

On the other hand, we are looking at a relatively compelling UPST's estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.27x, based on the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 45.52x, the annualized FQ1'25 adj EPS of $1.20 (+3.4% QoQ/ +196.7% YoY), and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $3.00 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +35.72%).

This is compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.35x, LC at 0.36x, SOFI at 1.03x, PYPL at 1.67x, and XYZ at 5.07x, underscoring why UPST's profitable/ high-growth cadence may remain attractive upon a moderate pullback to our Buy zone, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is UPST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

UPST's Overbought Levels

For now, UPST has already charted an impressive recovery by +110.8% since the recent April 2025 bottom, with the stock seemingly on its way to retesting the next resistance levels of $70s after running away from its 50/100/200-day moving averages.

Given the massive rally, it is unsurprising that the stock has also entered overbought levels by the time of writing, albeit with historical trends seemingly suggesting a durable upward momentum ahead.

On the other hand, readers must note that there may be increased potential for volatility, attributed to UPST's higher short interest volumes by +26.9% since the 2025 bottom and the rather exuberant stock market sentiments, with a pullback rather likely upon normalization in sentiments.

This is especially true since the Fed has turned hawkish in the last FOMC meeting, with it remaining to be seen how the tariff war may impact the US economy and the consumer spending trends in H2'25 once the 90-day pause ends by early July 2025.

This may be well-balanced by the One Big Beautiful Bill's supposed "ability to avert a sharp fiscal contraction in 2026," despite the risks arising from the drastically higher "federal deficit over the next decade."

For now, the intermediate-term uncertainties are why we do not believe in chasing UPST at current inflated levels, attributed to the higher dollar cost averages and the potential capital losses.

Based on the 1Y P/E mean of 40x (nearer the peer group mean of ~32x) and the annualized FQ1'25 adj EPS of $1.20 (+3.4% QoQ/ +196.7% YoY), it is apparent that the stock is trading at a notable premium to our updated fair value estimates of $48.00 as well.

While there remains an excellent upside potential of +63.5% to our bull-case long-term price target of $120, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $3.00, we believe that interested investors may be better off waiting for a moderate retracement for an improved margin of safety.

UPST 4Y Stock Price

Based on UPST's historical price cycle since the H1'23 bottom, we are likely to see the stock hit new heights before pulling back as traders take their gains, with a retracement to the December 2023 support levels of $47s likely in the intermediate term.

Given the potential -35.9% downside from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

UPST is a Hold here.

data.title: Village Super Market: Store Replacements Continue To Boost The Bottom Line
data.description:
Village Super Market (NASDAQ:VLGEA) continues to rally, and the reason for this may go beyond just the recent broad market rally among micro-cap and small-cap stocks. As discussed in my past coverage of VLGEA stock, this New Jersey-based operator of ShopRite-branded supermarkets has benefited greatly from closing down older locations, replacing them with new stores.

As seen in the company's latest quarterly earnings release, this strategy continues to pay off, given the impact of VLGEA's new store openings on revenue and earnings growth. Although this grocer's valuation is in line with its peers, especially when you take into account VLGEA's smaller size, status as a controlled company et cetera, there's likely still a path for shares to climb higher, due to both earnings growth and multiple expansion.

Furthermore, compared to other micro-cap/small-cap opportunities out there, VLGEA may not appear to be the most appealing, but perhaps is worthy of consideration as a defensive play, given the possibility of the current market melt-up giving way to a new round of volatility.

VLGEA Stock: Recap and Recent Quarterly Results

As discussed in my last write-up on Village Super Market, and as hinted-at above, the bull case here is pretty cut-and-dry. Over the last two fiscal years, VLGEA has experienced a steady increase in both revenue and earnings.

Again, a big factor behind this is the company's continued focus on replacing aged locations with new, fresh stores. Since early 2024, Village has opened two such locations. First, in March 2024, Village Super Market opened a replacement ShopRite store in Old Bridge, NJ. Second, last April, Village opened another ShopRite replacement location in Watchung, NJ.

Along with the replacement store strategy, Village Super Market has embraced digital ordering, another factor behind's improved fiscal performance. Before, there may have been concern that these two catalysts would have a sort of "one and done" effect on VLGEA's performance.

However, based on the company's latest quarterly results, it's clear they are still having an impact. On June 3, Village Super Market released results for its third fiscal quarter, ending April 26, 2025. Thanks to a 1.9% increase in same-store sales, coupled with the aforementioned new store openings, revenue increased 3.1% year-over-year, from $546.4 million to $563.7 million.

Thanks to the impact of increased sales, coupled with a slight decrease in operating costs, plus the benefit of increasing patronage dividends from Wakefern, the retail cooperative and ShopRite trademark owner that Village is a member of, earnings came in at 75 cents per share, up 25% from the 60 cents in EPS reported during Q3 FY2024.

For the nine-month period ending Apr 26, 2025, revenue was up by nearly 3.8%, with diluted earnings per share up by around 17% compared to the prior year's comparable nine-month period. Unfortunately, management doesn't provide much in the way of guidance in the company's quarterly earnings. Village Super Market also does not hold quarterly earnings calls.

So, is it uncertain as to what the future holds? Maybe, maybe not. It should be noted that the latest store opening happened just 17 days before the last day of the fiscal quarter. Hence, much of the new store sales growth was likely driven by the Old Bridge, NJ location opened last year, rather than the Watchung, NJ location opened this year.

In other words, the impact of this new location could be more pronounced in the quarters ahead, perhaps enabling Village Super Market to again report strong year-over-year results during the current fiscal quarter, which ends this month. If this occurs, VLGEA shares may be in for further price appreciation later in 2025.

VLGEA Valuation

At current prices, VLGEA trades for 10.3 times trailing twelve month (TTM) earnings, and at a TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of around 7.4x. Competitors to VLGEA, including Albertson's (ACI), Kroger (KR), and Weis Markets (WMK) trade at much higher earnings multiples. ACI trades for 13.4x TTM earnings, while KR and WMK sport TTM earnings multiple in the high-teens.

On an EV/EBITDA basis, however, VLGEA doesn't look particularly cheap. ACI, KR, and WMK all trade at EBITDA multiples of 7x-8x, in line with the valuation of Village Super Market. However, as noted before, Village Super Market's total enterprise value may be skewed, as outstanding debt and lease liabilities are countered by a hidden asset.

Yes, if you've read my past coverage of VLGEA stock, you'll know that in the past I've used an adjusted enterprise value for valuation purposes, by treating the company's outstanding loans to the Wakefern cooperative as equivalent to cash, and deducting them from the enterprise value calculation.

However, much like another "hidden asset" of Village's, the company's real estate portfolio, it's not as if the company is going to redeem all of these tomorrow, and use the cash to buy back shares or pay down debt. These loans are perhaps necessary, to sustain the operations of the retail cooperative.

Speaking of which, the fact that Super is a Wakefern/ShopRite member, this status is also a likely contributor to VLGEA's valuation discount on a P/E basis. The Wakefern cooperative, which provides product sourcing, advertising, and other essential services to Village Super Market, ending this partnership would likely cause major disruptions.

More importantly, this status as a ShopRite member likely precludes the company from ever being acquired by a major chain. In theory, another large Wakefern member could eventually acquire VLGEA, but there's only one member of this cooperative larger than that of Village Super Market.

Add in the fact that VLGEA is controlled via Class B voting shares held by the founding Sumas, and there's still much to suggest that a major re-rating is just around the corner.

That said, I reiterate my view that a further rise in price for this stock remains within the realm of possibility. Here's why.

Why Village Shares May Still be En Route to $50+ Per Share

Over the past year, VLGEA's earnings have increased by double-digits, with the replacement store opening in Old Bridge, New Jersey contributing to this growth. In the fiscal quarters ahead, the newly-opened Watchung, NJ location could have a similar impact.

Not only that, Village Super Market has another replacement ShopRite location pending, in East Orange, New Jersey. A newly-built ShopRite is slated to be the retail anchor at The Crossings at Brick Church Station, a mixed-use development in East Orange.

Unfortunately, there's not much publicly-available news out there about when this development is slated to open. The developer's website still touts an "expected delivery" this year. Still, whether the Brick Church Station location opens this fall, or maybe early next year, it would still likely have an impact on earnings.

These newly-built locations feature more modern features than the old-school ShopRites they are replacing, including a greater amount of floor space devoted to higher-margin premade and specialty items. As has occurred with the prior replacement stores, this points to further earnings growth.

Even if there are diminishing returns from replacement store openings, the opening of two new locations between now and the start of the next fiscal year could translate into another year of double-digit earnings growth.

TTM earnings right now stand at around $3.80 per share, add 10% to this, and we get annualized earnings of around $4.18 per share. At VLGEA's current earnings multiple, this suggests a valuation in the low-to-mid $40s.

Again though, do not discount the potential for additional, if only slight, multiple expansion. Since the growth resurgence first took shape, VLGEA has re-rated, from an earnings multiple in the high single-digits, to where it stands today.

In the past, the market has been willing to give this stock a valuation in the low-to-mid teens range. If re-rated to 12x on further earnings growth, VLGEA would be trading for over $50 per share.

Bottom Line on Village Super Market Stock

Beyond the latest store opening, and the upcoming store opening, it's possible that Village Super Market has another replacement store in the works. Per VLGEA's latest 10-Q filing, when discussing capital expenditures over the past nine months, Village Super Market references making a purchase of the real estate at its Springfield, NJ store:

While not for certain, this suggests that the company's Springfield, NJ location could eventually be redeveloped/rebuilt as a replacement location. A cursory Google Maps search of the store in question confirms that this is an older-style store ripe for a revamp.

If Village's ShopRite locations, which are primarily located in the New Jersey suburbs of New York City, continue to be replaced/modernized, the company may in turn continue to generate a steady increase in earnings. Far from the "value trap" VLGEA once was in the past, the stock may be well-positioned to continue performing well.

That said, as I've noted before, if the growth resurgence trend weakens, or if growth stagnates once again, the market may respond, perhaps even overreact, by sending shares back to their prior bargain-basement valuation.

Nevertheless, as the recession-resistant nature of grocery stores may help sustain the store revamp trend, irrespective of what happens to the economy in the coming years. Hence, there may be value in considering this stock as a defensive play among small-caps.

With this, consider VLGEA stock a buy at current prices.

data.title: Lessons Learned From My Development Days: 2 Shovel-Ready REIT Picks
data.description:
As many of my readers on Seeking Alpha know, I was a developer for over two decades, during which I built a variety of properties for clients such as Walmart, O’Reilly Auto Parts, Sherwin-Williams, Dollar General, PetSmart, and others.

I knew at an early age that I wanted to become a developer because it was in my DNA.

When I finished college, I decided to go to work for a small-town developer who had considerable land holdings in the county. I decided to work for him because I knew that I could gain valuable experience across property sectors such as shopping centers, office, net lease, and warehousing.

Unfortunately, towards the end of the business partnership, I gained insight into the riskier lodging sector, as my former partner decided to construct a hotel and conference center in our town. He was undercapitalized for the project, and that ultimately was the one torpedo that sank the ship (our partnership).

Until that point, my business partner and I co-developed dozens of successful properties, some making more money than others. Although I was not experienced in risk mitigation, I learned how to make each project more valuable by practicing trial and error.

For example, my very first lease contract was a gross contract in which the landlord was responsible for paying roof and parking lot maintenance, in addition to taxes and insurance (although there was a cap).

I remember getting a little snow removal bill, which was my responsibility, and this led me to always negotiate triple net leases where the tenant is responsible for all costs (taxes, insurance, and maintenance).

Another mistake I made early in my career was negotiating cost-plus contracts with general contractors. If you’re not familiar with this type of agreement, it simply means that the general contractor gets reimbursed for all expenses, plus an additional amount of profit.

In some cases (rare in my opinion), cost-plus contracts can help reduce costs, but in one of my larger projects, it led to an expensive $500,000 cost overrun that “your truly” had to eat. I now remind people that your contractor is only as good as your contract.

Over the years, I learned to add more contingency to every new deal, both soft costs (engineering, architectural, and finance costs) and hard costs (construction), and to always allow for weather delays.

I remember building a small shopping center in Boone, NC, for Advance Auto Parts and Sherwin-Williams. Before commencing construction, I was very excited about this project because I had two 15-year leases with high-quality tenants, and the initial going yield was around 11%.

However, I did not factor in the weather and the fact that the local municipality was going to be a pain in the butt. After closing on the land, it took months to get a permit to commence construction, and then I was further delayed with massive snow, which was especially challenging because the paving plans were closed for months and months.

I had a $2 million construction loan with interest ticking away with a burn rate of around $12k per month. It didn’t take long for the 11% development yield to become 9%. I was in Boone a few weeks ago, and I drove by to snap a photo of the project:

I sold the property to a friend of mine, and he has enjoyed the rent checks for over two decades. I regret not owning the property because it’s located on a high-barrier-to-entry site, but I remind myself frequently that I have 15,000 rent checks coming in every month with my stake in Realty Income (O).

There is little doubt that development can be risky, but for experienced investors, there can be a significant opportunity to capitalize on the wealth creation process “from the ground up.”

Currently, I’m working on ground-up projects in Costa Rica, South Carolina, and possibly Poland and the UK. Each of these is with experienced colleagues who have decades of experience within their respective markets.

However, for readers here on Seeking Alpha, I wanted to shine a light on two promising opportunities that have plenty of curb appeal. So, without further ado, let’s unroll the blueprint.

A Sunbelt Gem

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP) is an industrial REIT that owns 536 properties or around 63 million square feet of multi-tenant “flex” space across infill and supply-constrained submarkets. The company prefers to own last-mile e-commerce locations and shallow bay buildings (from 20K to 140K SF) as opposed to larger box buildings (140K SF and above).

So EGP’s customers rent space ranging from 20,000 SF to 100,000 SF, which makes the portfolio highly diversified – the top 10 customers represent just 7.1% of annualized base rent. 75% of EGP’s revenue is generated from tenants that lease under 100,000 SF.

In addition to its diversified tenant exposure, EGP also has an attractive geographic footprint with investments targeted in high-growth Sunbelt markets with an emphasis on local economies that are growing faster than the U.S. as a whole.

For example, EGP has around $100 million of assets in my hometown of Greenville/Spartanburg (6 buildings), including the buildings below at Access Point.

As of February 11th, EGP’s operating portfolio was 96.5% leased and 95.7% occupied by tenants with approximately 1,600 leases.

EGP has a development history, having constructed 269 of its own buildings since 1996 (representing 49% of the portfolio). These development properties total over 31 million SF with a total investment of $3.2 billion.

Although development is a riskier business model as opposed to acquiring stabilized assets, EGP has done a terrific job managing its balance sheet. As of Q1'25, the company’s debt to total market capitalization was just 13.7%, with debt to EBITDA of just 3.2x.

Take it from me, as a developer for over twenty years, having a fortress balance sheet can mitigate development risk considerably, and EGP has been a very conservative capital allocator. In fact, I would argue that EGP has one of the best-managed balance sheets in the entire REIT sector.

In January, the company refinanced a $100 million unsecured term loan, reducing the credit spread by 30bps, resulting in savings of approximately $1.5 million over the remaining 5 years of the term. In March, the company repaid a $50 million unsecured term loan and only has $95 million of debt maturing for the remainder of the year.

I always like to see how a company performs during adversity. As seen below, EGP did decrease adjusted funds from operations, or AFFO, per share during the Global Financial Crisis (by 26% in 2010), it has since generated 10% CAGR over the last 15 years.

While I always like to see a company consistently boosting its dividend, EGP did not cut its dividend in 2009 or 2010, and as you can see below, the company has raised its dividend by 10% CAGR over the last 10 years. This demonstrates a steady hand within the EGP's management team.

In Q1'25, EGP generated FFO per share of $2.12 that exceeded the midpoint of guidance, an increase of 7.1%. In addition, the company provided guidance for Q2'25 of $2.13 to $2.21 per share and $8.81 to $9.01 for the year, and these midpoints represent increases of 5.9% and 7.2% compared to the prior year.

EGP shares are trading at 25.1x, which represents a satisfactory margin of safety compared with the company’s historical multiple of 30.3x. The dividend yield is 3.3% with a payout ratio (based on AFFO per share) of 80%.

Given the Sunbelt focus and muted construction starts (hit a post-pandemic low of 34.7 million SF) that lead to less first-generation space, EGP should continue to grow its revenues. Analysts are also bullish, forecasting AFFO per share growth of 9% in 2026 and 12% in 2027. I see a bright future for EGP, and based on the FAST Graphs chart below, I’m targeting annualized returns of 25%.

Not Your Ordinary Office REIT

COPT Defense Properties (CDP) is not your traditional office REIT.

The company owns 195 properties (22.4 million SF) that consist of 164 office properties and 31 single-tenant data center shells. The locations are very close to key U.S. Government defense installations and missions (CDP refers to as Defense/IT Portfolio).

CDP’s tenants include the U.S. Government and their defense contractors, who are primarily engaged in priority national security activities and who generally require mission-critical and high-security property enhancements. In addition, CDP owns approximately 1,020 acres of land that has a build-out potential of approximately 11.0 million square feet.

CDP’s defense tenant portfolio achieves steady growth from high occupancy and tenant retention, as well as lower CapEx. The USG represents 35.8% of the portfolio, and other top tenants include General Dynamics (4.8%), Northrop Grumman I2.2%), Boeing (2.1%), CACI International (2.1%), Peraton (2.0%), and Booz Allen (1.8%).

One of the biggest moats around CDP’s business model is its land holdings, mostly in knowledge-based defense installations in Maryland, Northern Virginia, DC, Huntsville, and San Antonio.

Since 2012, CDP has focused its capital allocation on its Defense/IT Portfolio that supports priority U.S. Defense Missions (90% of Annualized Rent).

CDP has an active development pipeline with $308 million of active projects (756,000 SF) that are currently 62% leased. The company has a ~1.2 million SF development leasing pipeline and ~1.5 million SF of potential opportunities.

CDP can fund $250–$275 million of development/acquisition investment on a leverage-neutral basis (cash flow after dividend) with no outside equity required.

Similar to EGP (referenced above), CDP also has a rock-solid balance sheet with a significant unencumbered pool of assets (97%). Secured debt accounts for only 3% of CDP’s debt outstanding.

Keep in mind that vacancy leasing is tied to DOD cyber activity, which has been steadily growing over the past 10 years due to growth in DOD cyber funding. Also, CDP's retention rate (~80%) is more than double that of the Office REITs (~40%) that report this metric.

With that in mind, CDP’s earnings history, dating back to 2007, has been lumpy. I haven’t been as bullish until recently, when the company began to recycle its office portfolio.

As shown below, CDP’s dividend history has not been as impressive; however, over the last three years, the company has increased its dividend by 10.9%. In addition, it has strengthened its balance sheet by lowering the average interest rate by 70 bps and reducing the % of secured debt from ~12% to ~3%.

In Q1'25, CDP generated FFO per share of $0.65, at the midpoint of guidance, a 4.8% year-over-year increase. Also, the same property's cash NOI increased 7.1% year-over-year. Also, CDP maintained 2025 FFO per share guidance of $2.66 at the midpoint.

As shown below, CDP shares trade at 14.6x P/AFFO, below the normal multiple of 16.5x. The 4.3% dividend is well-covered with a payout ratio of 63%.

I’m very bullish on CDP’s defense-focused business model and the appropriated budget increases and bipartisan support for future growth in Defense Budgets (expected to continue to drive demand for existing and new development).

The ~1.2 million SF development leasing pipeline and ~1.5 million SF of potential opportunities should generate enhanced peer-leading growth in future years. As shown below (using the FAST Graphs forecasting tool), I’m estimating annualized returns of 15%;

In Closing

“You don't learn to walk by following rules. You learn by doing, and by falling over.” Richard Branson.

My experience as a real estate developer has taught me more than the wisdom that I could have obtained from reading textbooks. Over the years, I’ve learned to become more risk-averse, always seeking to avoid the same mistakes.

I think this is the reason that I’m one of the most-followed writers here on Seeking Alpha because readers want to learn from a battle-tested stock analyst who has overcome dark days by one simple word:

Perseverance

As always, thank you for reading and commenting below.

Have a great 4th of July.

God Bless America.
